news_summary: Scientists are making exciting advances in health by exploring innovative therapies like stem-cell treatments for frailty, spider silk for nerve repair, and diet strategies that boost fat burning, alongside creative approaches to healing through art.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques réalisent des avancées passionnantes dans le domaine de la santé en explorant des thérapies innovantes telles que les traitements à base de cellules souches pour lutter contre la fragilité, la soie d'araignée pour réparer les nerfs et les stratégies alimentaires qui stimulent la combustion des graisses, parallèlement à des approches créatives de guérison par l'art.
news_summary_es: Los científicos están logrando avances emocionantes en el campo de la salud mediante la exploración de terapias innovadoras, como los tratamientos con células madre para la fragilidad, la seda de araña para la reparación nerviosa y las estrategias dietéticas que estimulan la quema de grasas, junto con enfoques creativos para la curación a través del arte.

articles.title: Read an extract from Art Cure by Daisy Fancourt
articles.summary: In this extract from Daisy Fancourt's Art Cure, the March read for the New Scientist Book Club, we learn about how art classes transformed life for Russell after he had a stroke
articles.content:
In this extract from Daisy Fancourt's Art Cure, the March read for the New Scientist Book Club, we learn about how art classes transformed life for Russell after he had a stroke

Taking time to paint in Cornwall, UK Ashley Cooper/Alamy

Russell stood with his hand on the door, wondering whether to go in. It just wasn’t his scene. He was only here because his doctor had told him to come.

It had started with a stroke. He’d been walking home from work when the blood supply to the base of his brain became blocked and the world careened sideways. He’d had to relearn to walk, to talk. There had been months lying in bed staring at the ceiling and panicking about his future. He’d developed back pain so bad he couldn’t sit up. He’d lost his job, his relationship with his partner fell apart, he couldn’t play with his son any more, he put on weight, he couldn’t sleep. When he did sleep, his breathing kept stopping, so he had to wear a mask over his head at night, blowing air into his lungs to keep his airways open. His doctors prescribed dozens of pills, but new problems kept accumulating. He felt himself spiralling down into depression.

When his doctor first wrote the prescription for eight weeks of art classes, Russell thought for a moment it was some sort of joke. How was art supposed to help? But he didn’t feel he had anything to lose any more, so he opened the door.

Advertisement

The first class wasn’t as bad as he’d expected. He didn’t paint, just watched the others in the class. But somehow just being around the paints, the colours, and the calming atmosphere had an impact, and on his way home he noticed his breathing was slower and deeper and he felt a bit calmer than he had walking in. The next week wasn’t as daunting. He recognised a few of the other people. Again, he didn’t do any art. But that night he couldn’t sleep, so he snuck outside and sat in the shed in the garden, doodling little caricatures of the people in the art group. In the third class, he picked up a paintbrush. Russell’s still not sure how things escalated so rapidly after that, but a few weeks later, he found himself standing in front of the art group to announce an idea he’d had: to paint portraits of them all.

I first met Russell at 6 a.m. in the basement cafeteria of a Premier Inn in Manchester. We were both waiting to go on television for the BBC Breakfast news show to talk about a new proposal to roll “arts on prescription” across the National Health Service. Over cups of instant coffee, I tried to grasp the magnitude of the experience he was telling me about.

Free newsletter Sign up to Book Club Join our friendly crowd of fellow book club members in reading and discussing the latest in science and science fiction. Sign up to newsletter

At the next check-up, Russell explained, his doctor was pleased with his progress. His mood had improved and his pain levels were lower. Even his blood pressure was better. The art class gave structure to his week, and he found himself looking forward to it. At the following check-up they started to review his medications the doctor didn’t feel he needed as many. His sleep was better too.

As his portraits neared completion, Russell approached the Museum of Gloucester to ask if they would allow him to host an exhibition of the paintings in their café. He called the exhibition “We’re All Mad Here”. His classmates and his doctor were amongst those who attended. He received his first commission shortly afterwards – a nurse who had come to the exhibition wanted him to paint her children. That was just the beginning.

In the decade since, Russell Haines has exhibited his artwork all around the UK, from Gloucester Cathedral to the Tower of London. His pieces sell for thousands of pounds (if you can get your hands on one fast enough). He’s been leading his own classes back in the community too, and his doctor has been referring more patients to him. He doesn’t take any pills nowadays. He hasn’t even had to visit his doctor in over a year.

I asked him, “How great a change do you think those art classes actually made for you? How big of an impact are we talking?”

He didn’t miss a beat.

“They saved my life.”

This is an extract from Daisy Fancourt’s Art Cure: The science of how the arts transform our health (Cornerstone Press), the March read for the New Scientist Book Club. Sign up to read along with us here
articles.reasoning: The article describes a real-world, meaningful health improvement through art therapy for stroke recovery, showing broad potential benefits for patients and healthcare systems. It provides detailed context about the transformative impact on an individual's physical and mental health, illustrating a significant positive change.
articles.personality_title: How art classes helped a stroke survivor rebuild his life
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Russell had a stroke that affected many parts of his life. He struggled with walking, talking, pain, depression, and lost his job and family connection. His doctors tried many medicines, but he still felt worse.

**What happened** – Russell’s doctor suggested he attend eight weeks of art classes. At first, he was unsure and didn’t even paint. But the calming environment helped him relax. Gradually, he started painting and even held an art exhibition. Over time, his health and mood improved.

**Impact** – The art classes gave Russell a new purpose and helped reduce his pain and depression. His blood pressure improved, and he needed fewer medicines. Today, he is a successful artist who teaches others and no longer needs regular doctor visits.

**What's next step** – More patients like Russell may be offered “arts on prescription” programs through the National Health Service. This could help others recover in new ways beyond traditional medicine.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Attending art classes helped Russell recover from a stroke and change his life for the better.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment des cours d'art ont aidé un survivant d'AVC à reconstruire sa vie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Russell a eu un AVC qui a affecté plusieurs aspects de sa vie. Il avait des difficultés à marcher, à parler, souffrait de douleurs et de dépression, et a perdu son emploi et ses liens familiaux. Ses médecins ont essayé de nombreux médicaments, mais il allait toujours mal.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le médecin de Russell lui a proposé de suivre huit semaines de cours d'art. Au début, il hésitait et ne peignait même pas. Mais l'ambiance apaisante l'a aidé à se détendre. Peu à peu, il s'est mis à peindre et a même organisé une exposition. Avec le temps, sa santé et son humeur se sont améliorées.

**Impact** – Les cours d'art ont donné à Russell un nouveau but et ont aidé à réduire sa douleur et sa dépression. Sa tension artérielle s'est améliorée, et il a eu moins besoin de médicaments. Aujourd'hui, il est un artiste à succès qui enseigne aux autres et n'a plus besoin de visites régulières chez le médecin.

**Prochaine étape** – D'autres patients comme Russell pourraient bénéficier de programmes « arts sur ordonnance » via le Service National de Santé. Cela pourrait aider d'autres personnes à se rétablir autrement que par la médecine traditionnelle.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Suivre des cours d'art a aidé Russell à se remettre d'un AVC et à changer sa vie.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo las clases de arte ayudaron a un sobreviviente de un derrame cerebral a reconstruir su vida
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Russell sufrió un derrame cerebral que afectó muchas partes de su vida. Tenía dificultades para caminar, hablar, sentía dolor y depresión, y perdió su trabajo y conexión familiar. Sus médicos probaron muchos medicamentos, pero él seguía empeorando.

**Qué pasó** – El doctor de Russell le sugirió asistir a ocho semanas de clases de arte. Al principio dudaba y ni siquiera pintaba. Pero el ambiente tranquilo le ayudó a relajarse. Poco a poco comenzó a pintar e incluso organizó una exposición. Con el tiempo, su salud y estado de ánimo mejoraron.

**Impacto** – Las clases de arte le dieron a Russell un nuevo propósito y ayudaron a reducir su dolor y depresión. Su presión arterial mejoró y necesitó menos medicinas. Hoy es un artista exitoso que enseña a otros y ya no necesita visitas regulares al médico.

**Próximo paso** – Más pacientes como Russell podrían recibir programas de “arte con receta” a través del Servicio Nacional de Salud. Esto podría ayudar a otros a recuperarse de formas nuevas, más allá de la medicina tradicional.

**Frase clave** – Asistir a clases de arte ayudó a Russell a recuperarse de un derrame cerebral y cambiar su vida para mejor.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a quiet art studio corner with scattered paintbrushes and colorful palettes resting beside an open door, through which soft light spills onto a simple wooden easel holding a half-finished portrait, symbolizing healing and transformation through art after adversity.

articles.title: Scientists discover diet that tricks the body into burning fat without exercise
articles.summary: Researchers found that cutting two amino acids common in animal protein—methionine and cysteine—made mice burn significantly more energy. The boost in heat production was nearly as powerful as constant exposure to cold temperatures. The mice didn’t eat less or exercise more; they simply generated more heat in their beige fat. The discovery hints that diet alone might activate the body’s calorie-burning machinery.
articles.content:
Shivering in the cold is uncomfortable, but it does force the body to burn extra calories to stay warm. Research has consistently shown that cold exposure increases energy use in both mice and humans. This calorie burning through heat production is known as thermogenesis.

Many scientists and drug companies are trying to find ways to make the body behave as if it is cold, triggering thermogenesis without ice baths or freezing temperatures. But obesity researchers Philip Ruppert and Jan-Wilhelm Kornfeld from the Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology (BMB) at the University of Southern Denmark explored a different strategy.

Instead of lowering temperature, they investigated whether diet alone could switch on thermogenesis.

Their focus was on reducing two specific amino acids in food: methionine and cysteine.

After a series of experiments in mice, the team found that diet induced thermogenesis produced almost the same weight loss as constant exposure to five degrees Celsius around the clock. The study was conducted with BMB colleagues Aylin Güller, Marcus Rosendahl, and Natasa Stanic, and the results were published in the journal eLife.

Cutting Methionine and Cysteine Boosted Energy Burn

Over seven days, the researchers adjusted the levels of methionine and cysteine in the animals' diets. Mice that ate a diet low in these amino acids burned more calories than mice fed a standard diet.

"The mice that burned the most energy ate the same amount of food as the others, and they didn't move more or less. We saw a 20% increase in their thermogenesis. They lost more weight, and it was not because they ate less or exercised more -- they simply generated more heat," explains Jan-Wilhelm Kornfeld.

Kornfeld is a molecular biologist and a professor with the Danish Diabetes and Endocrine Academy (DDEA) at the Novo Nordisk Foundation Center for Adipocyte Signaling at BMB, University of Southern Denmark.

Methionine and cysteine are found in high amounts in animal based proteins such as meat, eggs, and dairy. They are present in much lower amounts in plant foods like vegetables, nuts, and legumes that are associated with healthy aging (as shown in this study). Because vegetarians and vegans avoid animal products, they naturally consume less methionine and cysteine than people who regularly eat meat.

Beige Fat Activation and Calorie Burning

The researchers also wanted to know where the extra calorie burning occurred. They found that it took place in beige fat, a type of fat stored just under the skin in both mice and humans. This same fat tissue is activated during cold exposure.

Fat was burned in beige fat during both cold induced thermogenesis and diet induced thermogenesis.

"This tells us that beige fat doesn't care whether the burning is triggered by cold or by diet," says Philip Ruppert.

Ruppert, a molecular biologist with a PhD, was at SDU when the study was performed and is now at Cornell University in New York.

"We know from other studies that vegetarians and vegans are, in several respects, healthier than meat-eaters. We haven't tested a methionine/cysteine-restricted diet in humans, only in mice, so we can't say for certain that the same effect would occur in people -- but it's absolutely a possibility," he says.

Potential New Obesity Treatments

The researchers believe the next step is to explore whether future obesity treatments could safely increase energy expenditure without requiring major lifestyle changes from patients.

They are also interested in developing functional foods that are naturally low in methionine and cysteine.

"It would also be interesting to study whether Wegovy patients experience additional weight loss if they switch to a diet without the amino acids methionine and cysteine -- in other words, a diet free of animal proteins," says Kornfeld.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery that a diet low in two amino acids can activate calorie-burning beige fat and increase energy expenditure without exercise, potentially leading to new obesity treatments. This has broad implications for public health given the global obesity epidemic and offers a non-invasive, diet-based approach to weight management. The study is detailed, focused, and presents a meaningful breakthrough with potential large-scale benefits.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find diet that helps burn fat by activating body heat
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Staying warm in the cold makes the body burn more calories through a process called thermogenesis. Usually, this happens when the body shivers or is exposed to cold temperatures. Scientists want to find ways to trigger this calorie burning without needing to feel cold.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Southern Denmark studied mice and discovered that reducing two amino acids, methionine and cysteine, found mostly in animal proteins, caused the mice to burn more calories. The mice did not eat less or exercise more, but their bodies generated more heat by activating beige fat, a special type of fat under the skin. This effect was nearly as strong as keeping the mice in cold temperatures all the time.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that diet alone might help the body burn calories by turning on beige fat. Since methionine and cysteine are mostly in meat, eggs, and dairy, eating less of these could boost calorie burning without extra exercise. This could lead to new ways to help people manage weight, especially for those who struggle with obesity.

**What's next step** – The scientists want to test if the same diet works in humans and explore making foods that naturally have less methionine and cysteine. They also want to see if people using weight-loss medicine might lose more weight by eating fewer animal proteins.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Reducing certain amino acids in the diet may help the body burn more calories by activating beige fat, offering a new approach to weight management without exercise.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent un régime qui aide à brûler les graisses en activant la chaleur corporelle
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Rester au chaud dans le froid pousse le corps à brûler plus de calories grâce à un processus appelé thermogenèse. Cela se produit généralement quand le corps frissonne ou est exposé au froid. Les scientifiques cherchent des moyens de déclencher cette combustion sans avoir à ressentir le froid.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université du Sud du Danemark ont étudié des souris et découvert que réduire deux acides aminés, la méthionine et la cystéine, présents surtout dans les protéines animales, faisait brûler plus de calories aux souris. Elles ne mangeaient pas moins ni ne faisaient plus d’exercice, mais leur corps produisait plus de chaleur en activant la graisse beige, un type spécial de graisse sous la peau. Cet effet était presque aussi fort que de garder les souris dans le froid tout le temps.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que le régime seul pourrait aider le corps à brûler des calories en activant la graisse beige. Comme la méthionine et la cystéine se trouvent surtout dans la viande, les œufs et les produits laitiers, en manger moins pourrait augmenter la combustion des calories sans exercice supplémentaire. Cela pourrait ouvrir de nouvelles façons d’aider à gérer le poids, surtout pour les personnes en surpoids.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent tester si ce régime fonctionne aussi chez l’humain et développer des aliments naturellement pauvres en méthionine et cystéine. Ils souhaitent aussi voir si les personnes prenant des médicaments pour perdre du poids pourraient perdre plus en mangeant moins de protéines animales.

**Une phrase clé** – Réduire certains acides aminés dans l’alimentation pourrait aider le corps à brûler plus de calories en activant la graisse beige, offrant une nouvelle approche pour gérer le poids sans exercice.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren una dieta que ayuda a quemar grasa activando el calor corporal
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Mantenerse caliente en el frío hace que el cuerpo queme más calorías mediante un proceso llamado termogénesis. Esto suele pasar cuando el cuerpo tiembla o está expuesto a bajas temperaturas. Los científicos buscan formas de activar esta quema de calorías sin tener que sentir frío.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad del Sur de Dinamarca estudiaron ratones y descubrieron que reducir dos aminoácidos, metionina y cisteína, que se encuentran principalmente en proteínas animales, hizo que los ratones quemaran más calorías. Los ratones no comieron menos ni hicieron más ejercicio, pero sus cuerpos generaron más calor activando la grasa beige, un tipo especial de grasa bajo la piel. Este efecto fue casi tan fuerte como mantener a los ratones en frío constante.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra que solo con la dieta el cuerpo podría quemar más calorías activando la grasa beige. Como la metionina y la cisteína están en carne, huevos y lácteos, consumir menos podría aumentar la quema de calorías sin ejercicio extra. Esto podría llevar a nuevas formas de ayudar a controlar el peso, especialmente para quienes tienen obesidad.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren probar si esta dieta funciona en humanos y explorar la creación de alimentos naturalmente bajos en metionina y cisteína. También quieren ver si las personas que usan medicamentos para bajar de peso podrían perder más si comen menos proteínas animales.

**Frase clave** – Reducir ciertos aminoácidos en la dieta puede ayudar al cuerpo a quemar más calorías activando la grasa beige, ofreciendo un nuevo método para controlar el peso sin ejercicio.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy, softly glowing scene of a small group of mice nestled among vibrant, stylized beige fat cells shaped like warm, gently pulsing embers, with scattered natural foods—nuts, legumes, and leafy greens—surrounding them, all rendered in warm earthy tones and gentle brushstrokes to evoke a sense of natural energy and quiet transformation.

articles.title: Daily briefing: Stem-cell treatment strengthens people with age-related frailty
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 26 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00672-zA single dose of stem cells can help older people with frailty build up their endurance. Plus, how to successfully make a career pivot and what ‘adulthood’ really means.
articles.content: Nature, Published online: 26 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00672-zA single dose of stem cells can help older people with frailty build up their endurance. Plus, how to successfully make a career pivot and what ‘adulthood’ really means.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough where a single dose of stem cells improves endurance in older people with frailty, representing a positive real-world impact on a broad and meaningful health issue affecting many elderly individuals. The article focuses on this single topic with sufficient detail to understand the achievement.
articles.personality_title: Stem-cell treatment boosts endurance in older people with frailty
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As people get older, many experience frailty, which means they feel weak and tired more easily. This makes it hard to do everyday activities and lowers their quality of life.

**What happened** – Scientists have found that giving a single dose of stem cells to older adults with frailty can help improve their endurance. This means these people can become stronger and more active after the treatment. The discovery was published in the scientific journal Nature on February 26, 2026.

**Impact** – This treatment is important because frailty affects many older people and currently has few effective options. By improving endurance, the stem-cell therapy helps older adults maintain their independence and carry out daily tasks more easily. It also opens the door for new ways to treat age-related health problems.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue studying this stem-cell treatment to understand how it works and to test it with more patients. They hope to make the therapy widely available and safe for all older adults who need it.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new stem-cell treatment shows promise in helping older people with frailty become stronger and more energetic with just one dose.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un traitement par cellules souches améliore l'endurance des personnes âgées fragiles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En vieillissant, beaucoup de personnes deviennent fragiles, ce qui signifie qu'elles se sentent plus faibles et fatiguées plus rapidement. Cela rend les activités quotidiennes plus difficiles.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont découvert qu'une seule dose de cellules souches peut aider les personnes âgées fragiles à améliorer leur endurance. Cela signifie qu’après le traitement, elles peuvent devenir plus fortes et plus actives. Cette découverte a été publiée dans la revue scientifique Nature le 26 février 2026.

**Impact** – Ce traitement est important car la fragilité touche beaucoup de personnes âgées et il existe peu de solutions efficaces. En augmentant l’endurance, la thérapie par cellules souches aide ces personnes à rester indépendantes et à mieux gérer leur vie quotidienne. Cela ouvre aussi la voie à de nouveaux traitements pour les problèmes de santé liés à l’âge.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier ce traitement pour mieux comprendre son fonctionnement et le tester sur plus de patients. Ils espèrent rendre cette thérapie accessible et sûre pour tous les seniors qui en ont besoin.

**Phrase clé** – Un nouveau traitement par cellules souches pourrait aider les personnes âgées fragiles à retrouver force et énergie grâce à une seule dose.
articles.personality_title_es: Tratamiento con células madre mejora la resistencia en personas mayores frágiles
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A medida que las personas envejecen, muchas se vuelven frágiles, lo que significa que se cansan y debilitan más fácilmente. Esto dificulta realizar las actividades diarias.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos descubrieron que una sola dosis de células madre puede ayudar a las personas mayores con fragilidad a mejorar su resistencia. Esto quiere decir que después del tratamiento pueden volverse más fuertes y activas. El hallazgo se publicó en la revista científica Nature el 26 de febrero de 2026.

**Impacto** – Este tratamiento es importante porque la fragilidad afecta a muchas personas mayores y hay pocas opciones efectivas. Al mejorar la resistencia, la terapia con células madre ayuda a que estas personas mantengan su independencia y puedan hacer sus tareas diarias con más facilidad. También abre nuevas posibilidades para tratar problemas de salud relacionados con la edad.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando este tratamiento para entender mejor cómo funciona y probarlo en más pacientes. Esperan que la terapia sea segura y esté disponible para todos los adultos mayores que la necesiten.

**Frase clave** – Un nuevo tratamiento con células madre promete ayudar a las personas mayores frágiles a ser más fuertes y activas con una sola dosis.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed warm-toned painting of a sturdy, ancient oak tree with its roots entwined around glowing, translucent stem cells that pulse gently with light, set against a soft, simple background of rolling hills symbolizing endurance and renewed strength.

articles.title: The silk solution: why surgeons are turning to spiders to repair nerve injuries
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Spider silk has always had a certain reputation: impossibly strong, faintly magical, and mostly reserved for nature documentaries and superhero origin stories. But in a humid lab in Oxford, it is being treated less like a wonder and more like a tool. A tool that might help surgeons solve […]
The post The silk solution: why surgeons are turning to spiders to repair nerve injuries first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Spider silk has always had a certain reputation: impossibly strong, faintly magical, and mostly reserved for nature documentaries and superhero origin stories. But in a humid lab in Oxford, it is being treated less like a wonder and more like a tool. A tool that might help surgeons solve a problem modern medicine still struggles with: getting severed nerves to reconnect across larger gaps.

Dr. Alex Woods, a trauma and orthopaedic surgeon for the NHS, is also the co-founder of a start-up called Newrotex. The setting he works in now looks a little different from an operating theatre: about 30 golden orb-web spiders sit in individual terrariums, spinning threads that researchers believe could guide nerve regeneration in a way the body often cannot manage on its own. Holding a fine strand between his fingers, Woods offers the simplest yet most poetic description of what they’re trying to do: “It acts like a scaffold for nerves to grow along like a rose on a trellis.”

It is, he says, a way to “bring this really cool ancient technology to patients.” The “ancient” part is doing a lot of work here, since spider silk is not new, not engineered, not invented. It is a biological material perfected over millions of years. The novelty lies in taking it seriously as a surgical device.

Why a nerve injury is not like a cut on the skin

When a nerve is cut, it does not simply knit itself back together in the way skin might. The body does attempt a repair by creating a temporary structure that the nerve can regenerate along, but that scaffold only survives for around 10 days before breaking down.

That short window becomes a major limitation when the gap is large. As Woods explains, “So if that gap is more than one centimetre with nerves regenerating at about one millimetre a day it can’t bridge big gaps and breaks down.” In other words, the nerve may still be trying to cross the injury when the bridge underneath it disappears. This is where the golden orb-web spider enters the chat.

The thread that matters most: drag-line silk

The spiders Newrotex works with are native to southern and eastern Africa, and the group in Oxford came from Madagascar. They are described as harmless to humans, though they are not especially sociable with each other. “They’re quite territorial so we keep them in their own terrariums and we keep the room very humid to mimic their natural environment,” Woods explains.

Their webs can be more than a metre wide, but the researchers are focused on a specific type of silk: drag-line silk, the thread spiders use to dangle and anchor themselves. Woods says this drag-line silk behaves like the body’s own nerve scaffold, “except it lasts for 150 days,” he says. “So now we can allow the nerves to get across the gap.”

Instead of relying on a structure that collapses after 10 days, the idea is to implant these silk fibres inside a vein or hollow conduit so they can serve as a longer-lasting guide. Over time, the fibres degrade in the body, meaning the scaffold can do its job and then exit the scene without demanding a permanent role.

Why this could be a big deal for recovery

Today, many nerve repairs require grafts, a technique that often means taking a nerve from elsewhere in the patient’s body and using it to bridge the damaged area. That approach can restore function, but it also creates a second injury site, and it can leave lasting numbness where the donor nerve was removed.

Helen Hide-Wright has experienced that trade-off firsthand. In 2022, she suffered a cardiac arrest while driving and crashed into a truck, breaking “almost every bone” in her body. She also severed nerves in her right arm, and surgeons had to remove nerves from behind her foot, leaving her without sensation there. She describes her treatment as successful, but still sees why a silk-based alternative is compelling.

“The surgery was brilliant,” she says, “but what Alex is offering would appear to be far more beneficial, a very exciting opportunity.”

Woods argues that a device that repairs a nerve immediately without needing a second operation site could also help with NHS costs. “It’s an extra operation site that has associated harm and that has a cost you won’t need if you have a device you can take and repair the nerve straight away,” he says.

The applications may stretch beyond trauma

Road accidents and severe injuries are an obvious starting point, but Woods is looking beyond that category. Nerve injuries can also occur during surgeries such as mastectomies or prostate cancer procedures, where nerves are sometimes damaged as an unintended consequence. These injuries can be difficult to treat, and the effects can be long-lasting.

“These are huge problems, which are nerve injuries which, right now, struggle to be treated,” Woods says. “So there’s a really exciting opportunity to take this simple device and open it up to people in all those different specialties.”

A promising idea still has to survive reality

For all the elegance of the science, Woods is candid about the distance between “works in principle” and “is available in hospitals.” Regulation, evidence thresholds, manufacturing demands, and cost can derail even strong medical concepts.

“I’m convinced that if my nerve was lacerated tomorrow I would have our implant put in,” he says. But, he adds, “Seeing all the steps you need to get through and the cost and the evidence you need to produce to bring that to patients has been really eye-opening, but there’s still a risk it’ll never see the light of day.”

Right now, the device is undergoing its first-in-human study in a hospital in Panama to establish safety, before further studies in the UK and the US.

If those steps go well, one of nature’s most famous materials may end up with a new reputation that is less superhero fantasy and more useful medicine.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article describes a novel medical innovation using spider silk to improve nerve injury repair, addressing a significant challenge in medicine with potential broad impact on patient recovery and healthcare costs. It provides detailed context about the problem, the solution, and ongoing clinical trials, indicating substantive progress toward a meaningful health breakthrough.
articles.personality_title: Surgeons use spider silk to help repair nerve injuries
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Nerve injuries are hard to fix because nerves don’t heal like skin. When a nerve is cut, the body builds a temporary path for it to grow back, but this path only lasts about 10 days. If the nerve gap is too big, the nerve can’t cross before the path disappears.

**What happened** – A surgeon named Dr. Alex Woods and his team at Newrotex are using silk from golden orb-web spiders to help nerves grow across bigger gaps. These spiders produce a special silk called drag-line silk, which lasts about 150 days inside the body. The silk acts like a scaffold, guiding the nerve to grow back properly. The silk is placed inside a vein or tube to support the nerve until it heals.

**Impact** – This new method could change how nerve injuries are treated. Today, doctors often have to take nerves from other parts of the patient’s body to repair damaged nerves, which causes extra harm and numbness where the nerve was taken. Using spider silk could avoid this second injury and might reduce surgery costs. It could help people injured in accidents or those who suffer nerve damage during other surgeries.

**What's next step** – The spider silk device is currently being tested in a hospital in Panama to make sure it is safe for humans. If successful, further studies will happen in the UK and the US. After that, it might become a common tool in hospitals for nerve repair.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists are using spider silk as a natural, long-lasting guide to help nerves heal better and avoid extra surgeries.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les chirurgiens utilisent la soie d'araignée pour réparer les lésions nerveuses
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les lésions nerveuses sont difficiles à réparer car les nerfs ne guérissent pas comme la peau. Quand un nerf est coupé, le corps crée un chemin temporaire pour sa régénération, mais ce chemin ne dure qu’environ 10 jours. Si l’écart est trop grand, le nerf ne peut pas traverser avant que le chemin disparaisse.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le chirurgien Dr Alex Woods et son équipe de Newrotex utilisent la soie d’araignée d’or pour aider les nerfs à repousser sur de plus grandes distances. Ces araignées produisent une soie spéciale appelée soie de fil d’attache, qui dure environ 150 jours dans le corps. Cette soie sert de support pour guider la repousse du nerf. Elle est placée à l’intérieur d’une veine ou d’un conduit creux pour soutenir le nerf pendant sa guérison.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle méthode pourrait changer le traitement des lésions nerveuses. Aujourd’hui, les médecins doivent souvent prélever un nerf ailleurs dans le corps pour réparer la zone endommagée, ce qui cause un deuxième dommage et un engourdissement. L’utilisation de la soie d’araignée pourrait éviter cette deuxième blessure et réduire les coûts chirurgicaux. Cela pourrait aider les personnes blessées dans des accidents ou ayant des lésions nerveuses après d’autres opérations.

**Prochaine étape** – Le dispositif en soie d’araignée est testé dans un hôpital au Panama pour vérifier sa sécurité chez l’humain. Si c’est concluant, d’autres études auront lieu au Royaume-Uni et aux États-Unis. Ensuite, il pourrait devenir un outil courant pour la réparation nerveuse.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques utilisent la soie d’araignée comme guide naturel et durable pour aider les nerfs à mieux guérir sans opérations supplémentaires.
articles.personality_title_es: Los cirujanos usan seda de araña para reparar lesiones nerviosas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las lesiones nerviosas son difíciles de reparar porque los nervios no sanan como la piel. Cuando un nervio se corta, el cuerpo crea un camino temporal para que crezca, pero este camino solo dura unos 10 días. Si la distancia es muy grande, el nervio no puede cruzar antes de que el camino desaparezca.

**Qué pasó** – El cirujano Dr. Alex Woods y su equipo en Newrotex usan seda de araña de orbe dorada para ayudar a los nervios a crecer a través de distancias mayores. Estas arañas producen una seda especial llamada seda de línea de arrastre, que dura alrededor de 150 días dentro del cuerpo. La seda actúa como un andamio para guiar el crecimiento del nervio. Se coloca dentro de una vena o conducto para apoyar al nervio mientras sana.

**Impacto** – Este método podría cambiar cómo se tratan las lesiones nerviosas. Hoy, los médicos suelen tomar nervios de otra parte del cuerpo para reparar el daño, lo que causa una segunda lesión y pérdida de sensibilidad. Usar seda de araña podría evitar esa segunda lesión y reducir costos de cirugía. Puede ayudar a personas heridas en accidentes o con daño nervioso tras otras cirugías.

**Próximo paso** – El dispositivo de seda de araña se prueba ahora en un hospital en Panamá para confirmar que es seguro en humanos. Si funciona, habrá más estudios en Reino Unido y Estados Unidos. Luego podría usarse comúnmente en hospitales para reparar nervios.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos usan seda de araña como guía natural y duradera para que los nervios sanen mejor sin necesidad de cirugías adicionales.
